title: If I Could Hold One Stock for the Next 5 Years, I'd Pick Google — Here's Why
subtitle: The cheapest mega-cap by a mile has the widest moat, the deepest pockets, and more shots on goal than any company on the planet.
summary: At 22x forward earnings with $110B+ in cash, Google is the most undervalued mega-cap in the Mag 7. Cloud is accelerating past a $45B run rate. Waymo is a real business. YouTube keeps compounding. And the DOJ antitrust overhang creates the best entry in years. Here's why GOOGL is my set-it-and-forget-it pick for the next half-decade.

links.text: Google Q1 2026 Earnings Beat Estimates on Cloud Strength
Waymo Raises $5 Billion at $30 Billion Valuation
YouTube Advertising Revenue Tops $9 Billion in Q1 2026
Google Gemini Drives Cloud Acceleration as AI Revenue Surges
DOJ Google Antitrust: What the Proposed Remedies Mean for Investors

bodyHtml:
Let me be direct: if someone told me I could only own one stock for the next five years and had to lock the door behind me, I'd pick Google. Not Nvidia. Not Microsoft. Not Amazon. Google.

Yes, the company that gets called a legacy search business loses AI to OpenAI and can't figure out social media. That Google. And I think that narrative is about to flip harder than most people realize.

The Valuation Gap Is Staring Everyone in the Face

Let's start with the math because the numbers do the talking here. Google trades at roughly 22x forward earnings. The Mag 7 median? Somewhere north of 30x. Apple is pushing 29x. Microsoft trades at 31x. Amazon at 35x+. Nvidia? Forget about it — still priced for perfection at 38x despite the pullback.

That gap is the whole thesis in a nutshell. Google is cheaper than every mega-cap peer by a wide margin, yet it owns three of the most powerful secular growth stories in tech. Cloud. Autonomous driving. AI. You're getting a conglomerate of high-growth businesses at a price that implies they don't exist.

And here's the kicker: Google has $110 billion in cash and marketable securities on the balance sheet. That's not just a safety net. That's a war chest. While other companies are borrowing to fund AI capex, Google can write checks from the sofa. Capital allocation matters over a 5-year horizon, and nobody in tech is better positioned to deploy capital aggressively than management sitting on that kind of ammo.

Google Cloud Is No Longer the Also-Ran

The most important number from the Q1 2026 earnings call wasn't search revenue or YouTube ad growth. It was Google Cloud revenue accelerating to a $45+ billion run rate, with operating margin expanding faster than analysts had modeled. This isn't the Cloud business that was losing money three years ago. This is a legit No. 3 — and gaining share against AWS and Azure.

What changed? AI. Specifically, Gemini. Enterprise customers are choosing Google Cloud for AI workloads because the TPU ecosystem offers better price-performance than Nvidia's GPUs for inference. And let's be honest — when you're a CIO looking at your cloud bill, price-performance is the only thing that matters. Google's custom silicon advantage is a real, durable moat that most investors still don't fully price in.

Cloud revenue grew 32% year-over-year in Q1 2026, and that's accelerating from the mid-20s we saw in 2025. At this trajectory, Cloud alone could be worth $300 billion+ as a standalone business by 2028. The market is still pricing it as a rounding error.

Waymo Is Finally a Real Business

I've been skeptical of autonomous driving for years — everyone should be. But Waymo has crossed the threshold from science experiment to actual revenue-generation machine. With a $30+ billion valuation after the latest funding round and commercial operations now running in eight major U.S. cities, Waymo is pulling away from Cruise and every Chinese competitor on safety data and scaling capability.

Waymo is currently doing over 150,000 paid rides per week. That's real revenue. Real unit economics. And the moat gets wider every quarter because miles driven = data collected, and data collected = better autonomy. Tesla talks about robotaxis. Waymo is actually dispatching them. Over a 5-year timeline, this business alone could be worth more than Google's entire market cap was a decade ago.

Think about it this way: Waymo's TAM is the entire transportation industry. Even capturing a fraction of that creates a multi-hundred-billion-dollar business. And the market is currently giving Waymo zero credit in GOOGL's valuation because it doesn't move the needle on next quarter's EPS. That's exactly the kind of optionality you want in a 5-year hold.

YouTube Keeps Printing Money

YouTube ad revenue hit $35+ billion in trailing twelve months — and it's still growing at double-digit rates. The CTV ad shift is real. YouTube now has the largest streaming audience in the U.S. by a wide margin, and advertisers are following the eyeballs. YouTube TV just passed 8 million subscribers. YouTube Shorts is taking market share from TikTok in the post-ban landscape.

This isn't a legacy business that's running on fumes. YouTube is compounding at a rate that would make most standalone companies jealous, and it gets the Google AI flywheel — better recommendations, better ad targeting, better creator tools. The Creator economy is only getting bigger, and YouTube is the infrastructure underneath it.

Addressing the Bear Case

I'm not going to pretend there aren't risks. There always are, and anyone who tells you a stock is risk-free is selling something.

The DOJ antitrust case is real. The remedy phase is ongoing, and the DOJ has proposed structural remedies including potential divestiture of Chrome and restrictions on default search agreements. This is the No. 1 overhang on the stock, and it's why GOOGL trades at a discount. My take? A forced Chrome sale would be a headline shock, but the economics of search aren't really in the browser — they're in the operating system defaults and the quality of the index. And Google has the best index by a mile. The worst-case scenario (forced breakup) would likely unlock value by letting the market separately value Search, Cloud, YouTube, and Waymo. Sum-of-the-parts analysis suggests breakup value north of $250 per share even in a conservative scenario.

AI competition from OpenAI and Meta is real. OpenAI is winning the narrative war. ChatGPT has consumer mindshare that Gemini hasn't matched yet. But here's what the narrative misses: Google has the distribution. Android. Chrome. Gmail. Google Workspace. When Google ships Gemini features into products that 3 billion people use daily, distribution wins every time. OpenAI doesn't have that moat. Meta doesn't have that moat. Nobody does except Google and maybe Apple.

An ad market slowdown is always a risk. If the economy softens, digital ad budgets get cut, and Google takes a hit along with everyone else. But Google's ad business is diversified across Search, YouTube, Display, and Network. And the shift toward Performance Max and AI-powered campaign optimization means advertisers get better ROAS — which makes them less likely to cut Google budgets in a downturn. The stickiness of search advertising is unmatched in the ad world. People cut brand ads first. They keep search ads running because those convert.

The Signal Take

Here's my original take, and the reason I'd bet on Google for the next five years: Google is the only mega-cap trading like a value stock while growing like a growth stock.

Every other Mag 7 member has either fully priced in its growth story (NVDA, MSFT, AMZN) or is facing existential disruption risk (META from AI, AAPL from hardware saturation). Google is the outlier. It's cheap because of antitrust fears and AI narrative loss. Both of those headwinds are temporary. The underlying business — the best monetization engine in human history, layered with the best research lab in AI, layered with a generational opportunity in autonomous driving — is absurdly durable.

Over five years, I expect Google Cloud to be a $80B+ business. I expect Waymo to be generating real EBITDA. I expect the DOJ saga to resolve in a way that doesn't structurally damage the business. And I expect the market to eventually re-rate GOOGL closer to its peer multiple.

At 22x earnings, you're not just buying a phenomenal business. You're buying a margin of safety that the rest of the Mag 7 doesn't offer. And in a market where every edge gets competed away, that's the best edge of all.

This article was written on May 31, 2026. The Signal is not a registered investment advisor. Do your own research.

meta.reasoning: Google is the most undervalued mega-cap by a mile. Cloud is accelerating past $45B run rate, Waymo is finally a real business, YouTube keeps growing, and they have $110B+ in cash. At 22x forward earnings vs. Mag-7 peers at 30x+, this is the value play hiding in plain sight.
meta.sourceHint: GOOGL Q1 2026 earnings, Google Cloud revenue acceleration, Waymo valuation rounds, Alphabet balance sheet, DOJ antitrust timeline
